The 'sorbitol' in plums and prunes is a sugar alcohol that has a natural laxative effect.
The world's largest producer of cherries is Turkey, followed by the United States and Chile.
The word 'cherry' comes from the Greek 'kerasos', meaning 'cherry tree'.
The world's most expensive cherry, the Sato Nishiki, is a premium variety from Yamagata, Japan.
The 'melatonin' in tart cherries may help regulate sleep patterns and improve sleep quality.
The world's largest producer of apricots is Turkey, followed by Uzbekistan and Iran.
The word 'apricot' comes from the Latin 'praecoquum', meaning 'early-ripening'.
The world's most expensive apricot, the Blenheim apricot, is an heirloom variety known for its intense flavor.
The 'amygdalin' in apricot kernels is a compound that can release cyanide if ingested in large amounts.
The world's largest producer of almonds is the United States, specifically California, accounting for over 80% of global supply.
The word 'almond' comes from the Old French 'almande', meaning 'almond'.
The 'alpha-tocopherol' in almonds is a form of Vitamin E that is highly bioavailable.
The world's largest producer of cashews is Vietnam, followed by India and Ivory Coast.
The cashew nut grows on the bottom of the cashew apple, which is also edible.
Raw cashews contain urushiol, the same toxin found in poison ivy, and must be roasted to be safe for consumption.
The world's largest producer of walnuts is China, followed by the United States and Chile.
Walnuts are high in alpha-linolenic acid (ALA), an essential omega-3 fatty acid.
The hard shell of a walnut is technically the endocarp of a drupe, not a true nut shell.
The world's largest producers of pistachios are Iran and the United States.
Pistachios are known as the 'smiling nut' in Iran and the 'happy nut' in China due to their partially open shells.
Pistachios are a good source of potassium and Vitamin B6, which are important for heart and nerve health.
The United States produces over 80% of the world's pecans, primarily in Georgia and Texas.
Pecans have the highest antioxidant content of any nut, according to some studies.
Turkey produces about 70% of the world's hazelnuts, primarily in the Black Sea region.
Hazelnuts are an excellent source of folate, which is essential for DNA synthesis and repair.
The world's largest producer of macadamia nuts is South Africa, followed by Australia and Kenya.
Macadamia nuts are native to Australia and were a staple food for Indigenous Australians for thousands of years.
Macadamia nuts have the highest fat content of any nut, mostly in the form of healthy monounsaturated fats.
The world's largest producer of Brazil nuts is Bolivia, followed by Brazil and Peru.
Brazil nuts are extremely high in selenium; just one or two nuts can provide the daily recommended intake.
Eating too many Brazil nuts can lead to selenium toxicity, known as selenosis.
Pine nuts are harvested from the cones of various species of pine trees.
Some people experience 'pine nut syndrome', a bitter metallic taste that can last for days after eating certain pine nuts.
The world's largest producer of peanuts is China, followed by India and Nigeria.
Peanuts are technically legumes, not nuts, and they grow their pods underground.
Peanut allergy is one of the most common and severe food allergies worldwide.
The world's largest producer of lentils is Canada, followed by India and Australia.
Lentils are a rich source of plant-based protein and iron, making them a staple in vegetarian diets.
The world's largest producer of chickpeas is India, accounting for over 60% of global production.
Chickpeas are also known as garbanzo beans, a name derived from the Spanish 'garbanzo'.
Chickpeas are high in fiber and folate, which support digestive and cardiovascular health.
The world's largest producers of quinoa are Peru and Bolivia.
Quinoa is a pseudocereal, meaning it is a seed used like a grain but is related to spinach and beets.
Quinoa is a complete protein, containing all nine essential amino acids that the body cannot produce on its own.
The world's largest producers of oats are Russia and Canada.
Oats are high in beta-glucan, a type of soluble fiber that can help lower cholesterol levels.
The world's largest producers of barley are Russia and France.
The world's largest producers of rye are Germany and Poland.
The world's largest producers of buckwheat are Russia and China.
Buckwheat is not a type of wheat; it is a seed related to rhubarb and sorrel.
The world's largest producers of millet are India and Niger.
The world's largest producers of sorghum are the United States and Nigeria.
Teff is a tiny grain native to Ethiopia and is the primary ingredient in injera bread.
Teff is the smallest grain in the world, about the size of a poppy seed.
Amaranth was a staple food of the Aztecs and was used in religious ceremonies.
The world's largest producers of strawberries are China and the United States.
Strawberries are not true berries; they are aggregate fruits with seeds on the outside.
The world's largest producers of blueberries are the United States and Canada.
Blueberries get their color from anthocyanins, which are powerful antioxidants.
The world's largest producers of raspberries are Russia and Mexico.
Raspberries are aggregate fruits, meaning they develop from multiple ovaries of a single flower.
The world's largest producers of blackberries are the United States and Mexico.
The world's largest producers of cranberries are the United States and Canada.
Cranberries grow on low-running vines in sandy bogs that are flooded for harvesting.
The world's largest producers of kiwifruit are China and Italy.
Kiwifruit was originally known as the Chinese gooseberry before being renamed for marketing purposes.
Kiwifruit was renamed in New Zealand in the 1950s after the country's national bird.
Mexico produces about 30% of the world's avocados, primarily in the state of Michoacán.
Avocados are technically large berries with a single large seed.
Avocados are high in monounsaturated fats, which are beneficial for heart health.
India produces about 40% of the world's mangoes, with over 1,000 varieties grown in the country.
The mango is known as the 'king of fruits' in India and is the national fruit of India, Pakistan, and the Philippines.
Mangoes are in the same botanical family as cashews and pistachios.
The world's largest producers of papayas are India and Brazil.
Papayas contain papain, an enzyme that can break down tough meat fibers, making it a natural tenderizer.
The world's largest producers of pineapples are Costa Rica and the Philippines.
A pineapple is a multiple fruit, meaning it is formed from the fusion of many individual flowers.
Pineapples contain bromelain, an enzyme that can digest proteins and is often used as a meat tenderizer.
The world's largest producers of coconuts are Indonesia and the Philippines.
A coconut is botanically a drupe, not a nut, despite its name.
The word 'coconut' comes from the Portuguese 'coco', meaning 'head' or 'skull', due to the three indentations on the shell.
The world's largest producers of dates are Egypt and Saudi Arabia.
Dates are one of the oldest cultivated fruits in the world, with evidence of cultivation dating back over 6,000 years.
The world's largest producers of figs are Turkey and Egypt.
Figs are technically inverted flowers, and they are often pollinated by specialized wasps.
The world's largest producers of pomegranates are India and Iran.
Pomegranates are high in punicalagins, which are extremely potent antioxidants found in the juice and peel.
The world's largest producers of olives are Spain and Italy.
Raw olives are extremely bitter due to a compound called oleuropein and must be cured to be palatable.
China produces over 90% of the world's spinach, primarily for the domestic market.
Spinach is high in iron, but it also contains oxalates, which can bind to the iron and hinder its absorption by the body.
The world's largest producers of kale are the United States and China.
Kale is exceptionally high in Vitamin K, which is essential for blood clotting and bone health.
The world's largest producers of broccoli are China and India.
Broccoli is technically a collection of edible flower buds that are harvested before they bloom.
The world's largest producers of cauliflower are China and India.
The world's largest producers of cabbage are China and India.
The world's largest producers of Brussels sprouts are the Netherlands and Mexico.
Brussels sprouts are named after the city of Brussels, Belgium, where they were popular as early as the 16th century.
The world's largest producers of asparagus are China and Peru.
The distinct smell in urine after eating asparagus is caused by the breakdown of asparagusic acid into sulfur-containing compounds.
The world's largest producers of artichokes are Italy and Egypt.
The edible part of an artichoke is the immature flower bud, specifically the heart and the base of the bracts.
The world's largest producers of celery are China and the United States.
The idea that celery is a 'negative calorie' food is a myth, although it is very low in calories and high in water.
The world's largest producers of carrots are China and Uzbekistan.
Carrots were originally purple or yellow; the orange variety was bred in the Netherlands in the 17th century.
Carrots are high in beta-carotene, which the body converts into Vitamin A for healthy vision and immune function.
The world's largest producers of potatoes are China and India.
Potatoes are native to the Andes mountains in South America and were first cultivated by the Inca people.
Potatoes are botanically tubers, which are thickened underground stems, not true roots.
China produces over 70% of the world's sweet potatoes, primarily for animal feed and starch production.
Sweet potatoes are exceptionally high in Vitamin A, primarily in the form of beta-carotene.
The world's largest producers of radishes are China and Japan.
Radishes are in the same botanical family as mustard, cabbage, and broccoli.
The world's largest producers of beets are Russia and France.
The deep red color of beets comes from pigments called betalains, which have antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties.
China produces over 70% of the world's cultivated mushrooms.
Mushrooms are fungi, not plants, and they lack chlorophyll, meaning they cannot produce their own food through photosynthesis.
Mushrooms are the only non-animal food source of Vitamin D, which they produce when exposed to UV light.
Truffles are rare, edible fungi that grow underground in symbiotic relationships with the roots of certain trees.
The characteristic aroma of truffles comes from complex volatile compounds, including dimethyl sulfide.
Iran produces over 90% of the world's saffron, the most expensive spice by weight.
Saffron consists of the dried stigmas of the Crocus sativus flower, with about 75,000 flowers needed to produce one pound of spice.
The yellow color of saffron comes from a carotenoid pigment called crocin.
The world's largest producers of milk are India and the United States.
Casein and whey are the two primary proteins found in milk, with casein making up about 80% of the total protein.
The world's largest producers of cheese are the United States and Germany.
There are over 2,000 distinct varieties of cheese produced worldwide, ranging from fresh to aged and soft to hard.
Rennet, an enzyme complex found in the stomachs of ruminant animals, is traditionally used to coagulate milk for cheese making.
The world's largest producers of yogurt are China and the United States.
Yogurt is produced by the bacterial fermentation of milk, primarily using Lactobacillus bulgaricus and Streptococcus thermophilus.
The world's largest producers of butter are India and the United States.
Butter is a water-in-oil emulsion, consisting of water droplets dispersed in a continuous phase of milk fat.
The world's largest producers of eggs are China and the United States.
Lecithin, a phospholipid found in egg yolks, is a powerful natural emulsifier used in making mayonnaise and hollandaise sauce.
Salt was so valuable in ancient times that it was used as currency, and the word 'salary' comes from the Latin 'salarium', or salt money.
Black pepper was once so expensive that it was known as 'black gold' and was used as a form of tax and ransom.
High consumption of added sugars is linked to an increased risk of obesity, type 2 diabetes, and heart disease.
Honey should never be given to infants under one year old due to the risk of infant botulism from Clostridium botulinum spores.
The characteristic flavor of maple syrup comes from the concentration of sugars and the Maillard reaction during the boiling process.
Coffee is a rich source of antioxidants, including chlorogenic acid, which may have various health benefits.
All true teas (black, green, white, oolong) come from the leaves of the same plant species, Camellia sinensis.
Most wine is produced from varieties of a single grape species, Vitis vinifera, which is native to the Mediterranean region.
Beer is one of the oldest prepared beverages in the world, with evidence of brewing dating back to ancient Mesopotamia.
Dark chocolate contains flavonoids, which are plant compounds that may have heart-protective effects.
China produces about 50% of the world's apples, with the Fuji variety being the most popular.
Apples are members of the Rosaceae family, making them relatives of roses, pears, and plums.
The world's largest producers of bananas are India and China.
Bananas are technically berries, while strawberries and raspberries are not.
The world's largest producers of oranges are Brazil and China.
Oranges are a hybrid between a pomelo and a mandarin orange.
The world's largest producers of grapes are China and Italy.
The United States produces about 30% of the world's corn (maize), primarily in the 'Corn Belt' of the Midwest.
Corn is a member of the Poaceae family, making it a type of grass.
The world's largest producers of rice are China and India.
Rice is the staple food for more than half of the world's population, primarily in Asia.
The world's largest producers of wheat are China and India.
Gluten is a group of proteins found in wheat that provides elasticity and structure to bread dough.
Soybeans are a complete protein source, meaning they contain all nine essential amino acids.
The cashew 'nut' is actually the seed of the cashew fruit, which grows outside the fruit itself.
The Maillard reaction is a chemical reaction between amino acids and reducing sugars that gives browned food its distinctive flavor.
Honey is the only food that doesn't spoil, thanks to its low moisture content and acidic pH.
The 'scoville scale' measures the pungency (spiciness) of chili peppers based on capsaicin concentration.
Fermentation is the metabolic process that converts sugar to acids, gases, or alcohol using microorganisms like yeast or bacteria.
The FDA's 'GRAS' list stands for 'Generally Recognized As Safe', a designation for food additives.
Quinoa is technically a seed, not a grain, making it a 'pseudocereal'.
The world's most expensive spice, saffron, comes from the dried stigmas of the saffron crocus flower.
Pasteurization is a heat treatment process that kills pathogenic bacteria in milk and other beverages.
Ethylene gas is a natural plant hormone used to trigger ripening in fruits like bananas and tomatoes.
The 'umami' taste was identified by Japanese chemist Kikunae Ikeda in 1908, linked to glutamate.
Canning was invented by Nicolas Appert in 1809 in response to a challenge from Napoleon to preserve food for his army.
Vitamin B12 is the only vitamin that contains a metal ion, cobalt.
The 'Nutrition Facts' label became mandatory in the United States following the Nutrition Labeling and Education Act of 1990.
Tempeh is a traditional Indonesian soy product made by fermenting soybeans with the fungus Rhizopus oligosporus.
Freeze-drying, or lyophilization, removes water from food by sublimation, preserving its structure and nutrients.
The 'smoke point' of an oil is the temperature at which it begins to break down and produce visible smoke.
Hydroponics is a method of growing plants without soil, using mineral nutrient solutions in a water solvent.
The 'allium' genus includes onions, garlic, leeks, and chives, all known for their sulfur-containing compounds.
Aflatoxins are poisonous carcinogens produced by certain molds that grow on soil, decaying vegetation, hay, and grains.
The 'Maillard reaction' was named after French chemist Louis-Camille Maillard, who first described it in 1912.
Lycopene is a bright red carotenoid hydrocarbon found in tomatoes and other red fruits and vegetables.
The 'Best Before' date on food labels refers to quality, while 'Use By' refers to safety.
Koji is a type of mold (Aspergillus oryzae) used in Japan to ferment soybeans for soy sauce and miso.
Irradiation is a food safety process that uses ionizing radiation to kill bacteria and parasites.
The 'brix' scale measures the sugar content of an aqueous solution, commonly used in the wine and fruit juice industries.
Vanilla is the only edible fruit of the orchid family, and it must be hand-pollinated in most regions.
The 'capsaicin' in chili peppers is concentrated in the white pith (placenta), not the seeds.
The 'Maillard reaction' occurs most rapidly between 140 and 165 °C (280 to 330 °F).
The first commercial frozen foods were sold by Clarence Birdseye in 1930.
Anthocyanins are water-soluble vacuolar pigments that may appear red, purple, or blue depending on the pH.
The 'Clean Label' movement encourages food manufacturers to use simple, recognizable ingredients.
Sauerkraut is made by a process of lactic acid fermentation similar to how traditional pickles are made.
Vacuum packaging extends the shelf life of food by removing oxygen, which inhibits the growth of aerobic bacteria.
The 'emulsifier' lecithin is found in egg yolks and soybeans and helps mix oil and water.
Aquaponics combines aquaculture (raising fish) with hydroponics (growing plants) in a symbiotic environment.
The 'brassicaceae' family, also known as cruciferous vegetables, includes broccoli, kale, and cauliflower.
The 'danger zone' for food safety is between 40°F and 140°F, where bacteria grow most rapidly.
The 'HACCP' system (Hazard Analysis and Critical Control Points) was originally developed for NASA to ensure food safety in space.
The 'Maillard reaction' is responsible for the crust on bread and the sear on a steak.
The first chocolate bar was made by Joseph Fry in 1847 by mixing cocoa powder, sugar, and cocoa butter.
Resveratrol is a polyphenol found in the skin of red grapes and is linked to various health benefits.
The 'Non-GMO Project' is a non-profit organization that provides third-party verification for non-GMO food.
Kombucha is a fermented tea drink produced by a symbiotic culture of bacteria and yeast (SCOBY).
Modified Atmosphere Packaging (MAP) replaces the air inside a package with a gas mixture to slow down spoilage.
The 'stabilizer' xanthan gum is produced by the fermentation of glucose or sucrose by the bacterium Xanthomonas campestris.
Precision agriculture uses GPS and IoT sensors to optimize crop yields and reduce resource use.
The 'solanaceae' family, or nightshades, includes tomatoes, potatoes, eggplants, and peppers.
Cross-contamination occurs when harmful bacteria are transferred from one food or surface to another.
The 'Maillard reaction' produces hundreds of different flavor compounds depending on the ingredients and temperature.
The first microwave oven was sold in 1947, following the accidental discovery of microwave heating by Percy Spencer.
Omega-3 fatty acids are essential fats that the body cannot produce on its own and must be obtained from food.
The 'Fair Trade' label ensures that farmers and workers in developing countries are paid a fair price for their products.
Kefir is a fermented milk drink made with kefir grains, which are a combination of bacteria and yeast.
The 'preservative' sodium benzoate inhibits the growth of potentially harmful bacteria, mold, and yeast in acidic foods.
High-Pressure Processing (HPP) uses extreme pressure to inactivate foodborne pathogens without using heat.
Vertical farming involves growing crops in vertically stacked layers, often in controlled indoor environments.
The 'cucurbitaceae' family includes cucumbers, melons, squashes, and pumpkins.
The 'five-second rule' is a myth; bacteria can contaminate food almost instantly upon contact with a surface.
The 'Maillard reaction' is inhibited by high acidity (low pH).
The first commercial tea bags were sold by Thomas Sullivan in 1908.
Probiotics are live microorganisms that provide health benefits when consumed, especially for gut health.
The 'Organic' label in the US is regulated by the USDA and requires strict adherence to specific farming practices.
Miso is a traditional Japanese seasoning produced by fermenting soybeans with salt and koji.
Nitrites are used in cured meats to prevent the growth of Clostridium botulinum and give the meat a pink color.
Extrusion is a food manufacturing process used to create products like breakfast cereals and pasta.
Regenerative agriculture focuses on restoring soil health and biodiversity to improve ecosystem resilience.
The 'rosaceae' family includes apples, pears, strawberries, raspberries, and stone fruits like peaches.
Handwashing is the single most effective way to prevent the spread of foodborne illnesses.
The 'Maillard reaction' is what makes toast taste different from plain bread.
The first carbonated soft drink was created by Joseph Priestley in 1767.
Prebiotics are specialized plant fibers that act as food for good bacteria in the gut.
The 'Country of Origin Labeling' (COOL) law requires retailers to provide information on the source of certain foods.
Kimchi is a staple Korean side dish made from salted and fermented vegetables, most commonly napa cabbage.
Sulfites are used as preservatives in wine and dried fruits to prevent browning and bacterial growth.
Homogenization is a process that breaks down fat globules in milk so they stay integrated rather than separating.
Cover crops are planted to protect and enrich the soil during the off-season, reducing erosion and nutrient loss.
The 'fabaceae' family, or legumes, includes beans, peas, lentils, and peanuts.
Salmonella is one of the most common causes of food poisoning, often linked to raw eggs and poultry.
The 'Maillard reaction' can produce acrylamide, a potential carcinogen, in starchy foods cooked at high temperatures.
The first commercial ice cream factory was opened by Jacob Fussell in Baltimore in 1851.
Antioxidants help protect cells from damage caused by free radicals, which are unstable molecules.
The 'Health Claims' on food labels must be supported by significant scientific agreement and authorized by the FDA.
Natto is a traditional Japanese food made from soybeans fermented with Bacillus subtilis var. natto.
BHA and BHT are synthetic antioxidants used to prevent fats and oils from becoming rancid.
Aseptic packaging involves sterilizing the food and the package separately and then filling them in a sterile environment.
Intercropping is the practice of growing two or more crops in close proximity to improve yields and soil health.
The 'poaceae' family, or grasses, includes major cereal crops like wheat, rice, corn, and barley.
E. coli is a bacterium that normally lives in the intestines of healthy people and animals, but some strains cause illness.
The 'Maillard reaction' is enhanced by the presence of water but inhibited by excessive moisture.
The first commercial chewing gum was sold by John B. Curtis in 1848.
Fiber is a type of carbohydrate that the body cannot digest, helping to regulate the body's use of sugars.
The 'Structure/Function Claims' on food labels describe the role of a nutrient in maintaining normal health.
Sourdough bread is made by the fermentation of dough using naturally occurring lactobacilli and yeast.
Propionic acid and its salts are used as preservatives in bread to prevent mold growth.
Spray drying is a method of producing a dry powder from a liquid or slurry by rapidly drying with a hot gas.
Crop rotation is the practice of growing different types of crops in the same area in sequential seasons.
The 'apiaceae' family includes carrots, celery, parsley, and parsnips.
Listeria monocytogenes is a bacterium that can grow at refrigeration temperatures and is a serious risk for pregnant women.
The 'Maillard reaction' is what gives roasted coffee its complex flavor profile.
The first commercial potato chips were sold by George Crum in 1853.
